overview.title: La decisión
overview.topic: Weighing whether to leave a stable job and start a company

notes.heading: Hace + time + que + present tense for ongoing duration
notes.body: 'Hace tres años que trabajo aquí' uses the present tense — not the present perfect — to express an action that started in the past and continues now. English uses 'I have been working here for three years' (perfect), but Spanish uses the present. This is a direct false-friend trap. The construction is: hace + time expression + que + present indicative.

notes.heading: Present perfect for recent or life-spanning experience
notes.body: The present perfect (he + participio) is used when the past action remains relevant to or connected with the present. Key triggers: 'nunca', 'alguna vez', 'siempre', 'hoy', 'este año', 'últimamente', and superlatives like 'lo más intenso que he vivido'. In Peninsular Spanish this is a strong rule; in Latin American Spanish the preterite often replaces it, so both registers are worth knowing.

notes.heading: Contrary-to-fact si-clause: imperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect
notes.body: 'Si fuera más valiente, habría fundado mi empresa' is the canonical contrary-to-fact conditional structure. Si + imperfect subjunctive states the hypothetical condition (which is false); conditional perfect states what would have already happened as a result. This differs from the open conditional (si + present indicative + future) where the condition is genuinely possible. The two structures must not be mixed.

notes.heading: Para cuando + subjunctive + future perfect
notes.body: 'Para cuando cumpla cuarenta años, habré ahorrado suficiente' combines two rules. First: para cuando referring to a future event always takes the present subjunctive — never the future indicative. Second: the result clause uses the future perfect (habré + participio) to show the saving will be completed before the milestone. Both parts of the construction are required; using the future indicative after para cuando is a clear grammatical error.

notes.heading: Conditional for hypothetical present outcomes
notes.body: The conditional (haría, quedaríamos) expresses what would happen under hypothetical circumstances. It is the consequence half of a si-clause structure, but can also stand alone to soften assertions or express politeness. In this story it contrasts with the future tense: the future (hablaré) expresses real planned intention; the conditional (haríamos) expresses what would happen in an unreal scenario.

sentences.translation: I have been working at this company for three years, but lately I have felt that something has to change.
rationale: trabajo → presente: hace + time expression + que + present tense describes an action that started in the past and is still ongoing — 'I have been working here for three years.'
he sentido → pretérito perfecto compuesto (present perfect): a recently completed experience with relevance to the present moment — the feeling has built up and still matters now.
tiene → presente: states a current general truth or condition, not a past event.

sentences.translation: My colleagues have told me that they have never had such a stable job, and that I would be wise to stay.
parts: un trabajo tan estable, y que
rationale: han dicho → pretérito perfecto compuesto: colleagues have said this on various occasions up to now — a repeated past action with present relevance.
han tenido → pretérito perfecto compuesto: their claim about never having had such a stable job spans up to the present, linking past experience to the current argument.
haría → condicional: expresses what I would do — a hypothetical outcome contingent on staying, not a certain fact.

sentences.translation: However, if I were braver, I would have already founded my own company.
rationale: fuera → subjuntivo imperfecto: si + imperfect subjunctive opens a contrary-to-fact conditional — I am not braver, so this is hypothetical.

habría fundado → condicional perfecto: the consequence of a contrary-to-fact past condition — what would have already happened by now if things had been different. Together, fuera + habría fundado form the classic si-clause perfect conditional structure.

sentences.translation: By the time I turn forty, I will have saved enough money to try it.
rationale: cumpla → subjuntivo presente: para cuando + a future reference always requires the present subjunctive in Spanish — the turning-40 event is anticipated but not yet real.
habré ahorrado → futuro perfecto: expresses an action that will have been completed by a specific future point. The saving happens before the milestone of turning 40.

sentences.translation: Next year, I will talk to a financial advisor and draw up a concrete plan.
rationale: hablaré → futuro simple: el año que viene signals a definite future intention — a planned action.
elaboraré → futuro simple: a second planned future action linked to the first, expressing firm intent.

sentences.translation: When that moment comes, I will know that I made the right decision because I have always believed in my dreams.
parts: que tomé la decisión correcta porque siempre
rationale: sabré → futuro simple: expresses certainty about a future state of knowing — what I will know when that moment arrives.

he creído → pretérito perfecto compuesto: siempre he creído describes a belief that began in the past and extends continuously up to the present moment, reinforcing that the conviction is still alive now.
